content.text: Connecting Kubernetes Clusters to Azure Arc

Azure Arc-enabled Kubernetes connects any CNCF-conformant Kubernetes cluster — on-premises, edge, EKS, GKE, OpenShift, k3s — to the Azure ARM control plane. The connection installs a set of agent pods in the azure-arc namespace: clusteridentityoperator (manages the cluster's MSI token), config-agent (polls GitOps Flux configurations), kube-aad-proxy (translates Entra ID tokens to Kubernetes auth), and resource-sync-agent (mirrors Kubernetes resources back to ARM). All connections are outbound TCP 443 from the cluster — no inbound rules required on the cluster firewall. Once connected, the cluster is a Microsoft.Kubernetes/connectedClusters ARM resource and can be targeted by Azure Policy, GitOps (Flux v2), Defender for Containers, and Container Insights.

attrs.alt: Arc-enabled Kubernetes: azure-arc agent namespace, extension pods, and ARM capabilities (Policy, GitOps, Monitor, Defender, Custom Locations)

content.text: Any CNCF-conformant Kubernetes cluster can be Arc-connected — including Amazon EKS, Google GKE, Red Hat OpenShift, Rancher RKE, and single-node k3s. The Arc agent adds about 400MB RAM overhead to the cluster via the azure-arc namespace pods.
AKS vs Arc-Enabled K8s
Aspect
AKS (Azure-managed)
Arc-enabled K8s (any distribution)
Cluster management
Azure manages control plane
You manage control plane; Arc manages ARM overlay
Supported distributions
AKS only
Any CNCF-conformant: EKS, GKE, RKE, OpenShift, k3s, kubeadm
Arc agent overhead
Not applicable (built-in)
~400MB RAM for azure-arc namespace pods
GitOps (Flux v2)
Supported via k8s-configuration
Supported via k8s-configuration (same API)
Azure Policy
OPA Gatekeeper extension
OPA Gatekeeper extension (same extension)
Defender for K8s
Defender for Containers
Defender for Containers via Arc extension
kubectl access
az aks get-credentials
az connectedk8s proxy (Entra ID token tunnel)
Cost
AKS control plane + node costs
Arc agent: free; extensions may have cost
Connecting a Cluster

Before running 'az connectedk8s connect', ensure the kubectl context is pointing to the target cluster and the cluster is reachable from the machine running the command. Register the three required resource providers (Microsoft.Kubernetes, Microsoft.KubernetesConfiguration, Microsoft.ExtendedLocation). The connect command installs the azure-arc namespace via Helm — it takes 2-5 minutes. Verify all pods in azure-arc namespace reach Running state before enabling extensions. The cluster shows connectivityStatus=Connected in ARM after the resource-sync-agent establishes the outbound connection.

# Connect a K8s cluster to Azure Arc
# kubectl context must point to the target cluster before running az connectedk8s connect

# Prerequisites
az provider register --namespace Microsoft.Kubernetes
az provider register --namespace Microsoft.KubernetesConfiguration
az provider register --namespace Microsoft.ExtendedLocation

# Install the connectedk8s CLI extension
az extension add --name connectedk8s
az extension add --name k8s-extension

# Verify kubectl context (cluster must be reachable from the machine running az)
kubectl cluster-info
kubectl get nodes

# Connect the cluster — installs Arc agent pods in azure-arc namespace
az connectedk8s connect \
  --name 'edge-cluster-prod' \
  --resource-group 'arc-k8s-rg' \
  --location eastus \
  --tags 'Environment=Production,Cluster=edge-cluster-prod,Owner=platform-team'

# Connection takes 2-5 minutes; verify agent pods are Running
kubectl get pods -n azure-arc
# Expected: clusteridentityoperator, config-agent, cluster-metadata-operator,
#           kube-aad-proxy, resource-sync-agent all Running

# Verify ARM resource was created
az connectedk8s show \
  --name 'edge-cluster-prod' \
  --resource-group 'arc-k8s-rg' \
  --query '{Name:name, Distribution:distribution, ConnStatus:connectivityStatus, K8sVersion:kubernetesVersion}' \
  -o json

# Verify cluster is Connected (takes up to 5 min after connect command)
az connectedk8s show \
  --name 'edge-cluster-prod' \
  --resource-group 'arc-k8s-rg' \
  --query 'connectivityStatus' -o tsv

# List all connected clusters across subscriptions
az graph query -q \
  "Resources
   | where type == 'microsoft.kubernetes/connectedclusters'
   | project Name=name, RG=resourceGroup, Distribution=tostring(properties.distribution),
             K8sVersion=tostring(properties.kubernetesVersion),
             Status=tostring(properties.connectivityStatus),
             NodeCount=toint(properties.totalNodeCount)" \
  --query 'data' -o table
RBAC and kubectl Access

Arc-enabled Kubernetes supports two RBAC layers. Azure RBAC (ARM) controls who can manage the Arc resource, enable extensions, and assign policies — roles like Azure Arc Kubernetes Admin and Azure Arc Kubernetes Viewer. Kubernetes RBAC (cluster-internal) controls access to namespaces and resources. Enable the azure-rbac feature to use Entra ID group OIDs directly in Kubernetes ClusterRoleBindings and RoleBindings — this eliminates the need for a separate kubeconfig distribution. Use 'az connectedk8s proxy' to get a temporary kubeconfig backed by Entra ID tokens for cluster administration.

# RBAC for Arc K8s: grant Entra ID users/groups access to cluster namespaces
# Two layers: Azure RBAC (ARM) and Kubernetes RBAC (cluster-internal)

# Layer 1: Azure RBAC — controls who can manage the Arc K8s ARM resource
# 'Azure Arc Kubernetes Admin' — cluster admin on the K8s cluster
# 'Azure Arc Kubernetes Viewer' — read-only across all namespaces
# 'Azure Arc Kubernetes Cluster User Role' — allows az connectedk8s proxy

az role assignment create \
  --role 'Azure Arc Kubernetes Admin' \
  --assignee '<platform-team-group-oid>' \
  --scope '/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/arc-k8s-rg/providers/Microsoft.Kubernetes/connectedClusters/edge-cluster-prod'

# Layer 2: Kubernetes RBAC — fine-grained namespace access
# The cluster must have --enable-azure-rbac to use Azure AD groups as K8s role bindings.
# Enable during connect (or via update):
az connectedk8s enable-features \
  --name 'edge-cluster-prod' \
  --resource-group 'arc-k8s-rg' \
  --features azure-rbac

# After enabling Azure RBAC, create ClusterRoleBindings with Entra ID group OIDs:
cat << 'EOF' | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
metadata:
  name: app-team-view
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: view
subjects:
- kind: Group
  name: '<app-team-entra-group-oid>'  # Entra ID group object ID
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
EOF

# Namespace-scoped RoleBinding for dev team on 'dev' namespace only
cat << 'EOF' | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: dev-team-edit
  namespace: dev
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: edit
subjects:
- kind: Group
  name: '<dev-team-entra-group-oid>'
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
EOF

# Use az connectedk8s proxy to get a local kubeconfig backed by Entra ID tokens
# Opens an HTTPS proxy on localhost:8443
az connectedk8s proxy \
  --name 'edge-cluster-prod' \
  --resource-group 'arc-k8s-rg' \
  --port 8443
# In another terminal: kubectl --server https://localhost:8443 get pods
Azure Policy for Arc Kubernetes

Azure Policy enforcement on Arc-enabled Kubernetes works through Gatekeeper OPA (Open Policy Agent), installed as a cluster extension (microsoft.policyinsights). When a policy is assigned to the Arc K8s resource, Gatekeeper constraints and constraint templates are automatically deployed to the cluster. Admission webhook enforcement runs locally in the cluster — policy violations are blocked at apply time without round-tripping to Azure. Policy compliance state is reported back to Azure Policy via the resource-sync-agent. Assign Kubernetes-specific built-in policies (no privileged containers, require resource limits, allow only approved images) at the cluster or resource group scope.

# Azure Policy for Arc K8s: enforce admission control via Gatekeeper OPA
# Policy effect 'auditIfNotExists' or 'deny' on K8s resource operations

# 1. Enable the Azure Policy extension on the cluster
az k8s-extension create \
  --name azure-policy \
  --cluster-type connectedClusters \
  --cluster-name 'edge-cluster-prod' \
  --resource-group 'arc-k8s-rg' \
  --extension-type microsoft.policyinsights \
  --scope cluster

# 2. Verify Gatekeeper is running
kubectl get pods -n gatekeeper-system
# Expected: gatekeeper-controller and gatekeeper-audit pods Running

# 3. Assign a built-in Kubernetes policy to the cluster
# 'Kubernetes cluster should not allow privileged containers'
az policy assignment create \
  --name 'arc-k8s-no-priv' \
  --display-name 'Deny privileged containers in Arc K8s' \
  --policy '/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyDefinitions/95edb821-ddaf-4404-9732-666045e056b4' \
  --scope '/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/arc-k8s-rg/providers/Microsoft.Kubernetes/connectedClusters/edge-cluster-prod'

# 4. Check compliance state
az policy state summarize \
  --resource '/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/arc-k8s-rg/providers/Microsoft.Kubernetes/connectedClusters/edge-cluster-prod' \
  --query 'results.nonCompliantResources' -o tsv

# 5. View Gatekeeper constraints generated by the policy
kubectl get constraints --all-namespaces
kubectl describe k8sazurecontainernoprivilege.constraints.gatekeeper.sh

# 6. Test policy enforcement (this should be denied)
cat << 'EOF' | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: test-priv-pod
  namespace: default
spec:
  containers:
  - name: test
    image: nginx
    securityContext:
      privileged: true
EOF
# Expected: Error from server: admission webhook denied — K8sAzureContainerNoPrivilege
Common Connecting Mistakes
Mistake
Consequence
Fix
Running az connectedk8s connect without a valid kubectl context
Command fails with 'no active context'; cluster not connected
Run kubectl cluster-info first to confirm context is valid and reachable
Not registering Microsoft.KubernetesConfiguration provider
Flux and Policy extensions fail to create
Register all three providers: Microsoft.Kubernetes, Microsoft.KubernetesConfiguration, Microsoft.ExtendedLocation
Deleting azure-arc namespace pods manually
Arc control plane loses connectivity; ARM shows Offline
Never delete arc-arc namespace — reconnect with az connectedk8s connect if needed
Using azure-rbac feature without Entra ID group OIDs in RoleBindings
All authenticated users get default permissions
Get group OIDs from az ad group show --group '<name>' --query id
Assigning policy at subscription scope before cluster is Connected
Policy assignment targets cluster before agent is ready; Unknown compliance
Wait for connectivityStatus == Connected before assigning policies
Mixing Arc K8s clusters with AKS clusters in same Flux configuration
Flux repo structure must handle both cluster types — subtle differences in Custom Locations
Use separate GitOps repos or branches for Arc K8s vs AKS clusters
Arc K8s Extension Management

Once a cluster is Arc-connected, enable capabilities by installing extensions via 'az k8s-extension create'. Each extension is a Helm chart deployed to a dedicated namespace on the cluster. Key extensions: microsoft.flux (Flux v2 GitOps), microsoft.azuremonitor.containers (Container Insights), microsoft.azuredefender.kubernetes (Defender for Containers), microsoft.policyinsights (Gatekeeper OPA), and microsoft.openservicemesh. Extensions support auto-upgrade within minor version bands. Use Resource Graph to identify clusters missing critical extensions and target them with DINE policies to install extensions automatically on new clusters.

# Arc K8s extensions: install and manage capabilities on connected clusters

# List all installed extensions on a connected cluster
az k8s-extension list \
  --cluster-type connectedClusters \
  --cluster-name 'edge-cluster-prod' \
  --resource-group 'arc-k8s-rg' \
  --query '[].{Name:name, Type:extensionType, State:installState, Version:version}' \
  -o table

# 1. Flux v2 GitOps extension
az k8s-extension create \
  --name gitops \
  --extension-type microsoft.flux \
  --cluster-type connectedClusters \
  --cluster-name 'edge-cluster-prod' \
  --resource-group 'arc-k8s-rg' \
  --scope cluster

# Verify Flux pods are running
kubectl get pods -n flux-system

# 2. Azure Monitor (Container Insights) extension
az k8s-extension create \
  --name azuremonitor-containers \
  --extension-type microsoft.azuremonitor.containers \
  --cluster-type connectedClusters \
  --cluster-name 'edge-cluster-prod' \
  --resource-group 'arc-k8s-rg' \
  --scope cluster \
  --configuration-settings \
    logAnalyticsWorkspaceResourceID='/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/<rg>/providers/Microsoft.OperationalInsights/workspaces/<ws>'

# 3. Defender for Containers extension
az k8s-extension create \
  --name microsoft-defender \
  --extension-type microsoft.azuredefender.kubernetes \
  --cluster-type connectedClusters \
  --cluster-name 'edge-cluster-prod' \
  --resource-group 'arc-k8s-rg' \
  --scope cluster

# 4. Open Service Mesh (traffic management, mTLS between pods)
az k8s-extension create \
  --name osm \
  --extension-type microsoft.openservicemesh \
  --cluster-type connectedClusters \
  --cluster-name 'edge-cluster-prod' \
  --resource-group 'arc-k8s-rg' \
  --scope cluster

# 5. Upgrade an extension to a specific version
az k8s-extension update \
  --name gitops \
  --cluster-type connectedClusters \
  --cluster-name 'edge-cluster-prod' \
  --resource-group 'arc-k8s-rg' \
  --auto-upgrade-minor-version true

# 6. Delete an extension (uninstalls the Helm chart from the cluster)
az k8s-extension delete \
  --name osm \
  --cluster-type connectedClusters \
  --cluster-name 'edge-cluster-prod' \
  --resource-group 'arc-k8s-rg' \
  --yes

# Resource Graph: find clusters missing the gitops extension
az graph query -q \
  "Resources
   | where type == 'microsoft.kubernetes/connectedclusters'
   | join kind=leftouter (
       Resources
       | where type == 'microsoft.kubernetesconfiguration/extensions'
       | where properties.extensionType =~ 'microsoft.flux'
       | project ClusterName=tostring(split(id, '/extensions/')[0])
     ) on $left.id == $right.ClusterName
   | where isnull(ClusterName)
   | project Name=name, RG=resourceGroup, Status=tostring(properties.connectivityStatus)" \
  --query 'data' -o table
Agent Upgrades and Cluster Maintenance

Arc K8s agents self-update by default within the minor version band. Check the current agent version with 'az connectedk8s show --query agentVersion'. If a cluster shows Offline status, inspect the azure-arc namespace pod logs — the most common causes are network connectivity loss to management.azure.com or *.his.arc.azure.com, or an expired MSI token (the clusteridentityoperator should renew automatically). Disconnect preserves the ARM resource but removes agents from the cluster; re-connecting with the same name and resource group reuses the existing ARM resource. Delete removes both agents and the ARM resource permanently.

# Arc K8s cluster maintenance: upgrade, disconnect, and re-connect

# 1. Upgrade the Arc K8s agent to latest version
#    Arc agents self-update by default; check current version:
az connectedk8s show \
  --name 'edge-cluster-prod' \
  --resource-group 'arc-k8s-rg' \
  --query 'agentVersion' -o tsv

# Manually trigger agent upgrade (for pinned versions)
az connectedk8s upgrade \
  --name 'edge-cluster-prod' \
  --resource-group 'arc-k8s-rg' \
  --upgrade-timeout 600

# 2. Check connectivity status and troubleshoot disconnected clusters
az connectedk8s show \
  --name 'edge-cluster-prod' \
  --resource-group 'arc-k8s-rg' \
  --query '{Status:connectivityStatus, AgentVersion:agentVersion, LastConnected:lastConnectivityTime}' \
  -o json

# If status is Offline: check azure-arc pods and network connectivity
kubectl describe pods -n azure-arc
kubectl logs -n azure-arc -l app=clusteridentityoperator --tail=50

# Test connectivity to required Arc K8s endpoints
ENDPOINTS=(
  'management.azure.com'
  'login.microsoftonline.com'
  '*.ods.opinsights.azure.com'
  'guestnotificationservice.azure.com'
)
for ep in "${ENDPOINTS[@]}"; do
  echo -n "$ep: "
  curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' --max-time 5 "https://${ep//\*./management.}" || echo 'TIMEOUT'
done

# 3. Disconnect a cluster (removes azure-arc namespace; preserves ARM resource)
az connectedk8s disconnect \
  --name 'edge-cluster-prod' \
  --resource-group 'arc-k8s-rg'
# ARM resource remains; status shows Offline
# Re-connect by running az connectedk8s connect again with same name

# 4. Delete the Arc K8s ARM resource (removes both Arc agent and ARM resource)
az connectedk8s delete \
  --name 'edge-cluster-prod' \
  --resource-group 'arc-k8s-rg' \
  --yes

# 5. Verify agent is removed from the cluster after delete
kubectl get ns azure-arc
# Expected: Error from server (NotFound): namespace 'azure-arc' not found

# 6. Health check: verify all clusters are Connected via Resource Graph alert rule
# Create an Azure Monitor alert on Resource Graph query:
az graph query -q \
  "Resources
   | where type == 'microsoft.kubernetes/connectedclusters'
   | where properties.connectivityStatus != 'Connected'
   | project Name=name, RG=resourceGroup, Status=tostring(properties.connectivityStatus),
             LastConnected=tostring(properties.lastConnectivityTime)" \
  --query 'data' -o table
Summary

Arc-enabled Kubernetes connects any CNCF-conformant cluster to ARM by installing azure-arc namespace agents via Helm. Register Microsoft.Kubernetes, Microsoft.KubernetesConfiguration, and Microsoft.ExtendedLocation providers first. The connect command requires a valid kubectl context pointing to the cluster. Once Connected, the cluster can be targeted by Azure Policy (Gatekeeper OPA via the azure-policy extension), GitOps via Flux v2 (via microsoft.flux extension), Defender for Containers, and Container Insights. Enable the azure-rbac feature to use Entra ID group OIDs in Kubernetes RBAC bindings. Use 'az connectedk8s proxy' for temporary Entra ID-backed kubectl access. Never manually delete pods in the azure-arc namespace.
